title: Codex Micro — OpenAI's first hardware is a keyboard
voiceStyle: Narrate briskly and crisply, like a sharp launch-day tech news explainer — energetic, clear diction, minimal pauses, never slurred:

scenes.voiceSegment: OpenAI — the company behind the famous AI chatbot ChatGPT — just shipped its first hardware ever. A tiny keyboard.
The Codex Micro: two hundred thirty dollars, made to control Codex — OpenAI's AI that writes code for you.
The big dial sets how hard the AI thinks. Simple task? Keep it fast. Hard one? Crank it up.
It can run several jobs at once — and six keys glow with each one's status: thinking, working, waiting, done.
Flick the joystick and it starts a job: review code, or fix a bug. One key approves. One rejects.

scenes.label: 13 keys · limited run · SOLD OUT — OpenAI × Work Louder
scenes.voiceSegment: Thirteen keys, two hundred thirty dollars, a limited run — and it's already sold out.
OpenAI separately bought a secret gadget company from the iPhone's designer — reportedly six and a half billion. This isn't it.

scenes.text: you won't type code — you'll *manage* it
scenes.voiceSegment: Here's the real story: coders are becoming managers of AI workers. This is the first desk console for that job.

scenes.text: Would YOU buy a keyboard for an AI?
scenes.voiceSegment: The box says: you can just build things. Would you buy a keyboard for your AI?
